text.title: microservices-design Core Rules
text.summary: 7 testable rules grounding the microservices-design methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Each service owns its data. No shared tables across services; cross-service reads go through APIs or events, never direct DB queries.
rule.rationale: Shared tables couple services through schema; one migration breaks N services.

rule.statement: Services never import each other's code directly. Cross-service interactions go through transport contracts (HTTP/gRPC/async).
rule.rationale: Code imports re-create the monolith inside a service mesh.

rule.statement: Failures in one service must not cascade. Use circuit breakers + timeouts + bulkheads on every inter-service call.
rule.rationale: Without circuit breakers, one slow service propagates latency to the entire mesh.

rule.statement: Async messaging is the default for cross-service workflows that span more than two services; sagas (orchestration or choreography per ADR) coordinate multi-step transactions.
rule.rationale: Synchronous chains amplify latency and failure surface.

rule.statement: Every service ships with health/liveness/readiness probes + structured logs + tracing instrumentation (OpenTelemetry). No service is observable-by-accident.
rule.rationale: Distributed systems are unobservable without explicit instrumentation.

rule.statement: API contracts are versioned (URL path / Accept header / proto package). Breaking changes ship a new version; old version is supported for >=N days.
rule.rationale: Lockstep deployment across services destroys the independent-deployment property.

rule.statement: Skip when the input does not satisfy the Applies-If preconditions or another methodology owns this output shape.
rule.rationale: Prevents duplicate work and wrong-tool application.
